title: Ankara Summit Delivers Defense Pacts, Patriot License Raises More Questions Than Answers, China Unveils 100GW Microwave Weapon
subhead: NATO's Ankara summit produced over $50 billion in defense procurement deals and a Patriot production license for Ukraine, but left the fundamental question of American commitment unanswered. Meanwhile, China publicly revealed a 100GW microwave weapon, Ukraine's drone war against Russian logistics escalated sharply, and India commissioned its sixth indigenous stealth frigate in 18 months.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Defense Deals Without Commitments
sections.body:
The NATO summit in Ankara concluded with a visible display of alliance unity and over $50 billion in defense procurement and defense-industrial pacts, but left the central strategic question — the trajectory of American disengagement from European security — firmly unresolved. According to the Kyiv Independent's analysis, the Pentagon's six-month posture review announced on June 18 remains pending, and previously announced force reductions stand unchanged: 5,000 troops withdrawing from Germany, the cancellation of the rotational brigade to Romania, and adjustments to U.S. contributions to the NATO Force Model.

The Defense Industry Forum produced a series of multinational procurement decisions: eleven allies selected the Saab GlobalEye to replace the U.S.-built E-3 early-warning fleet; Airbus A400M airlift and A330 tanker pooling arrangements were formalized; MQ-4C Triton maritime-surveillance procurement moved forward; the HALO space initiative and NATO Engine industrial network were launched; and allies committed over $40 billion for counter-drone capability. Germany is on course for the 3.5% core defense spending target by 2029. Poland secured a PAC-3 maintenance-hub signature but no commitment on permanent U.S. basing. The Baltic states won an upgrade of the Baltic Air Policing mission but not a permanent fighter presence.

President Trump again tied his demand for U.S. control of Greenland to the threat of further troop reductions, called Spain a poor contributor, and dismissed German defense spending as 'ridiculous.' The Czech Republic declined to contribute to the 70 billion euro ($79.9 billion) package for Ukraine, citing its own spending targets. Italy reportedly sought to soften language on the 2027 commitment, citing fiscal constraints.
sections.whyItMatters: The alliance is building European defense capacity at unprecedented speed, but the gap between procurement commitments and deployable capability remains years wide. Most of the Ankara deals are at inception stage with timelines extending to the end of the decade, while American force reductions are already underway.

sections.heading: Patriot License for Ukraine: A Long Road From Pledge to Production
sections.body:
President Trump's announcement at Ankara that the U.S. would give Ukraine a license to produce Patriot air-defense systems was the summit's most eye-catching weapons commitment — but the gap between the pledge and operational capability is measured in years, not months. According to AP and France 24 reporting, the license would not automatically allow Ukraine to manufacture complete Patriot batteries (launchers, radar, command posts, and missiles). It would more likely cover narrower elements: interceptor missiles, final assembly from imported kits, or production of selected components.

Timur Kadyshev of the University of Hamburg's IFHS told France 24 that it takes six months to a year just for the paperwork, because Patriot systems are under tight export controls. Only after that process does Lockheed Martin, the main manufacturer, grant the license. Japan is currently the only country besides the U.S. that produces Patriots on its territory; Germany's PAC-2 production was approved in 2022, with first missiles delivered in 2027, according to RUSI's Justin Bronk.

The immediate crisis remains unaddressed: Ukraine is running out of Patriot interceptors. On July 6, Kyiv's defenses failed to intercept any of 23 ballistic missiles Russia launched overnight, killing at least 22 people. The NATO Support and Procurement Agency announced a purchase of 700 PAC-2 and 200 PAC-3 missiles; Germany, Denmark, Norway, and Canada placed a joint order directly with the U.S. manufacturer. But global demand for interceptors is enormous, with South Korea, Japan, and Middle Eastern countries all replenishing stockpiles depleted by the Iran conflict. Interceptor seeker components face supply constraints.
sections.whyItMatters: The Patriot license is symbolically significant — it signals long-term Western commitment to Ukrainian air defense — but it will not change the battlefield equation for years. The binding constraint on Ukrainian air defense is not licensing but production capacity and component supply chains that are already stretched globally.

sections.heading: China Reveals 100GW Microwave Weapon, Signaling Anti-Satellite Ambitions
sections.body:
Chinese military scientists at the National University of Defense Technology (NUDT) published details this month of a high-power microwave (HPM) weapon system generating up to 100 gigawatts of microwave energy — the most extensive public disclosure to date of China's directed-energy weapons program. The research, led by Zhang Jun, was published in the journal High Power Laser and Particle Beams.

According to the South China Morning Post's reporting on the paper, the system combines multiple high-power pulse generators to create an unprecedented microwave beam. Researchers stated that pulsed-power technology has 'moved from laboratory prototypes to practical applications,' and that some gigawatt-class systems have already been delivered to the Chinese military. No independent verification of operational capability has been provided.

Defense experts note that even a 1GW microwave pulse can seriously damage satellite electronics in low-Earth orbit, making a 100GW system a theoretical anti-satellite threat of extraordinary magnitude — particularly aimed at constellations like Starlink. The paper also disclosed a lithium-ion capacitor hybrid system capable of instant activation and continuous power delivery at temperatures as low as -40°C, intended for electronic warfare in polar and extreme environments. Future priorities include reducing system size, cost, and improving beam accuracy for broader deployment.
sections.whyItMatters: The disclosure itself is a strategic signal. China rarely publishes details of advanced weapons programs; this public release is calibrated to demonstrate anti-satellite and counter-drone capability at a time of intensifying space and electronic warfare competition. Whether the 100GW system is operationally deployable or laboratory-grade remains unverified, but the declared intent to scale, miniaturize, and deploy across platforms is clear.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone War on Russian Logistics: 21 Tankers Hit in Sea of Azov
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone forces struck 21 Russian tankers, four tugboats, two dry cargo ships, and a dredger in the Sea of Azov overnight on July 10-11, according to Ukraine's General Staff. The operation, attributed to Ukrainian Defense Forces units including the drone commander known as Madyar, represents a dramatic escalation of Kyiv's naval drone campaign against Russian logistics.

According to RFE/RL, Russia temporarily halted all shipping through the Don-Azov Channel in response, with border guards notifying companies that passage requests through the Kerch Strait would not be accepted. Ukraine also struck the Ilsky refinery in Krasnodar (capacity 6.6 million tons of oil per year), the Ust-Luga refining complex in Leningrad region, and an oil depot in Rostov. Reuters reported that domestic gasoline output in Russia has fallen to around 65% of normal levels due to the strikes.

President Zelensky also established a new 'Long-Range Impact' Command within the armed forces, dedicated to concentrating long-range strike operations against Russian energy infrastructure and logistics. The command was published on the Presidential Office website and is intended to unify and intensify the drone and missile campaign targeting Russia's war-sustaining capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: The Sea of Azov strikes demonstrate that Ukraine's drone forces have achieved operational reach against naval logistics, forcing Russia to halt commercial traffic through a critical waterway. Combined with the new Long-Range Impact Command, this institutionalizes what had been ad hoc strike campaigns into a unified strategic effort — one that is visibly degrading Russia's domestic fuel supply.

sections.heading: Helsing's Secret German Drone Factory: AI Warfare at Scale
sections.body:
The New York Times reported on July 11 that German defense startup Helsing SE is manufacturing thousands of AI-powered drones for Ukraine at a secret facility in southern Germany. The factory operates under tight security: Helsing's name appears nowhere on the premises, employees sign non-disclosure agreements, and the facility can be dismantled and relocated within a day.

Since late 2024, the factory has delivered thousands of drones to Ukraine. The HX-2 strike drone, Helsing's primary product, uses AI for target recognition and engagement. TASS reported on the NYT disclosure, noting Russian accusations that European drone production for Ukraine makes those countries 'complicit in the killing of Russian children,' per Russia's Deputy UN Representative Maria Zabolotskaya.

Bloomberg reported in January that Ukraine had paused additional HX-2 orders due to problems in frontline testing, including a high failure rate on takeoff. Helsing disputed those findings. The disclosure of the factory's existence illustrates a broader shift in European defense: a startup-scale company producing mass autonomous weapons at a tempo and cost structure that traditional defense contractors cannot match.
sections.whyItMatters: Helsing's operation represents the industrialization of AI-powered drone warfare. A single German startup is now delivering thousands of autonomous weapons to an active conflict zone from a facility designed to be mobile and deniable. This is the template for how defense procurement is being disrupted across Europe.

sections.heading: India Commissions INS Mahendragiri: Sixth Stealth Frigate in 18 Months
sections.body:
India commissioned INS Mahendragiri, the sixth and final indigenous stealth frigate under Project 17A, at Visakhapatnam on July 12. Defence Minister Rajnath Singh presided over the ceremony, noting that six frontline frigates have been commissioned in the past 18 months — a pace reflecting India's accelerating defense indigenization.

INS Mahendragiri displaces approximately 6,670 tonnes and can reach 28 knots. Over 75% of its components are indigenous. It is armed with BrahMos surface-to-surface missiles, long-range surface-to-air missiles, a multifunction radar, indigenous rocket and torpedo launchers, an integrated anti-submarine defense system, an electronic warfare suite, and a close-in weapon system.

Singh emphasized that India's approach balances advanced technology with conventional military strength, stating that 'future wars may be fought with Artificial Intelligence, but they will still be won through national resolve, trained soldiers, and credible military power.' He also highlighted the Indian Navy's Operation Urja Suraksha, under which it escorted 18 merchant vessels carrying cargo worth over Rs 9,000 crore during the West Asia conflict, reinforcing India's role as a 'net security provider' in the Indo-Pacific.
sections.whyItMatters: Six indigenous frigates in 18 months is a striking demonstration of India's naval shipbuilding acceleration. The 75% indigenous content benchmark signals that India's defense manufacturing is moving from import dependency toward self-sustained production — a shift with implications for the Indo-Pacific balance and for global defense export competition.

sections.heading: North Korea Rejects Denuclearization, Vows to Bolster Nuclear Forces After NATO Summit
sections.body:
North Korea's foreign ministry issued a statement on July 11 rejecting the NATO Ankara summit's call for Korean Peninsula denuclearization, arguing that disarmament should begin with U.S. allies hosting American nuclear assets — specifically South Korea, Japan, and NATO nuclear-sharing members — rather than with Pyongyang.

The statement followed a separate KCNA report that North Korea had adopted measures to strengthen its nuclear forces 'quantitatively and qualitatively'. The U.S. intelligence community's 2026 Worldwide Threat Assessment assessed that North Korea is investing in nuclear-capable systems to deter the United States, challenge regional missile defenses, and improve the range and accuracy of its missile arsenal.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung, making his first NATO summit appearance, urged Secretary-General Mark Rutte to support efforts to curb North Korea's nuclear and missile development. The U.S., South Korea, and Japan reiterated their pledge for complete denuclearization and agreed to strengthen cooperation against Pyongyang's alleged cyber operations funding its weapons programs. North Korea has enshrined its status as a nuclear weapons state in its constitution since 2023 and has said it will only engage in talks if the U.S. recognizes it as a nuclear power.
sections.whyItMatters: The response is routine but marks another formal rejection of the diplomatic track. The invocation of U.S. nuclear-sharing arrangements as a precondition for any denuclearization discussion signals Pyongyang's intent to link its program to broader alliance dynamics — a framing that complicates any future arms control negotiation.

bottomLine: A consequential day for defense industry and military technology. The Ankara NATO summit generated over $50 billion in procurement commitments — from the Saab GlobalEye selection to $40 billion in counter-drone spending — but the alliance's most important deliverable, the Patriot license for Ukraine, will take years to translate into interceptors on the front line. China's public disclosure of a 100GW microwave weapon was a deliberate strategic signal about its anti-satellite and electronic warfare ambitions. Ukraine's drone forces demonstrated unprecedented naval reach by hitting 21 Russian tankers and forcing the closure of the Don-Azov Channel, while institutionalizing their campaign under a new Long-Range Impact Command. India's commissioning of its sixth indigenous frigate in 18 months illustrates that defense indigenization is no longer aspirational — it's operational. The pattern across all these developments is the same: the gap between strategic ambition and industrial reality remains the binding constraint.
sources: Kyiv Independent, AP, France 24, Reuters, RFE/RL, South China Morning Post, News24, EADaily, TASS, IBTimes, Sambad English, Firstpost, NPR, Yahoo News, MSN, Ukrinform, Boston Herald, International Business Times, KCNA
